---
title: "Welcome, Recap & Tooling Upgrade"
subtitle: "Session 1 — 12 March 2026"
author: "Prof. Dr. Claudius Gräbner-Radkowitsch"
institute: "Europa-Universität Flensburg"
date: "12 March 2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../../../assets/custom.scss]
    slide-number: true
    progress: true
    embed-resources: true
    footer: "Adv. Data Science & Econometrics 2026 | Session 1"
    width: 1280
    height: 720
---

## What is this course?

**Building on what you know:**
R, Quarto, tidyverse, basic linear regression — all from *Research Methodology* (Fall 2025)

**What we add:**

- Regression done properly: multiple predictors, binary outcomes, diagnostics
- Causal thinking: why correlation ≠ causation, and what to do about it
- Panel data, modern tools, professional communication

**The rule:** Every session starts with a **business question**, not a method name

::: {.fragment}
*Not:* "Today we learn logistic regression"
*But:* "Which firms are likely to exit the market — and can we predict it?"
:::

---

## Full course schedule {.smaller}

| # | Date | Title |
|:---:|---|---|
| 1 | Thu, 12 Mar | **Welcome, recap & tooling upgrade** ← today |
| — | 19 + 26 Mar | *Take-home task 1* |
| 2 | Thu, 09 Apr | Multiple regression: going beyond the basics |
| — | 16 + 23 Apr | *Take-home task 2* |
| 3 | Thu, 30 Apr | Modelling binary and categorical outcomes |
| 4 | Thu, 07 May | What can go wrong: biases and diagnostics |
| — | Thu, 14 May | *No lecture* |
| 5 | Thu, 21 May | Causation vs. correlation: thinking like an economist |
| 6 | Thu, 28 May | Panel data and fixed effects |
| 7 | Thu, 04 Jun | Coding smarter: R and AI tools |
| 8 | Thu, 11 Jun | Communicating data: advanced visualization |
| 9 | Thu, 18 Jun | Recap and looking ahead |

---

## Assessment

**Take-home tasks** (mandatory, pass/fail)

- Two structured analyses submitted as Quarto HTML reports
- Assigned during the conference/unavailable periods — that time is for you
- Submit via **GitHub Classroom** (link distributed today)
- Moodle fallback if GitHub push fails

| Task | Due | Topic |
|---|---|---|
| Task 1 | 02 April, 08:00 | Regression basics |
| Task 2 | 30 April, 08:00 | Extending a regression |

**Final exam**

- Open-book, on-site, individual
- Details confirmed later in the semester

---

## How this course works

**Where you code:** GitHub Codespaces — RStudio in your browser, zero installation

**Where you ask questions:** GitHub Discussions on the course repository
*(not email, not Moodle — so everyone benefits from the answer)*

**Where you submit:** GitHub Classroom for tasks; Moodle for official grades only

**What you need:**

- A GitHub account (free)
- GitHub Student Developer Pack (recommended — free professional tools)
- Nothing else

---

## Today's plan

| Time | Block |
|---|---|
| 16:00–16:20 | Welcome, course overview, assessment |
| 16:20–17:00 | Quarto upgrade: live demo document |
| 17:00–17:15 | Git orientation |
| 17:15–18:15 | Student exercise |
| 18:15–19:00 | Introduce Task 1, Q&A |

. . .

The exercise today is about **workflow setup**, not data analysis.
No dataset required — we are building the container before filling it.

---

## Quarto in this course

**Why Quarto matters:**

- Code and text in one place — no copy-pasting numbers into Word
- Results update automatically when data or code changes
- Renders to HTML, PDF, Word — same source file

**The standard we set today applies to every submission:**

- Proper YAML front matter
- Numbered sections, table of contents
- Bibliography with BibTeX citations
- Clean, commented code chunks

. . .

**Starting from Task 1, every submission is a rendered Quarto HTML file. Today you build that habit.**

---

## Git: the four things you need

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["📁<br/>Working directory"] -->|"Stage (Git pane)"| B["📋<br/>Staging area"]
  B -->|"Commit (+ message)"| C["💾<br/>Local repository"]
  C -->|"Push"| D["☁️<br/>GitHub (remote)"]
  D -->|"Pull"| A

  style A fill:#f4f6f8,stroke:#00395B
  style B fill:#f4f6f8,stroke:#69AACD
  style C fill:#f4f6f8,stroke:#00395B
  style D fill:#00395B,color:#ffffff,stroke:#00395B
```

**You only need these four actions.** No branching, no merging.

All of this happens in the **RStudio Git pane** — no command line.

::: {.fragment}
For a full introduction: [GitHub Skills — Introduction to GitHub](https://github.com/skills/introduction-to-github)
:::

---

## Take-home task 1

**You are an analyst producing a briefing on global development patterns.**

**Dataset:** World Bank — life expectancy and GDP per capita, 182 countries, 2017
Download from OSF: [osf.io/pguwf](https://osf.io/pguwf/)

**Five tasks:**

1. Load and explore — summary statistics, two plots
2. Simple regression: life expectancy ~ GDP per capita — interpret the coefficient
3. Log-transform GDP — does fit improve? Why?
4. Add one variable, compare models with a `modelsummary` table
5. Write a 150-word plain-language summary for a non-technical reader

**Deadline:** 02 April 2026, 08:00
**Submit:** GitHub Classroom *(link below)* | Moodle fallback

[GitHub Classroom link — to be added]{.muted}

---

## The break weeks

**19 March, 26 March:** I am at a conference — no lecture

This time is **intentional and structured:**

- Work on Take-home Task 1 at your own pace
- Review material from the prior course if anything feels rusty
- Post questions in **GitHub Discussions** — I will check regularly

. . .

::: {.callout-tip}
If something in Task 1 feels unfamiliar, that is a signal — not a problem.
The two weeks are designed for exactly this.
:::

---

## See you on 9 April

**Next session:** Thu, 09 April — *Multiple regression: going beyond the basics*

**Questions during the break?**
GitHub Discussions on the course repository *(link shared in Moodle)*

**Task 1 deadline:** 02 April 2026, 08:00

. . .

Good luck — and welcome to the course.

::: aside
**PDF export (Chrome/Edge):** click ☰ bottom-left → Tools → PDF Export Mode → print. Alternative: add `?print-pdf` to the URL, then print.
:::
